topic: BOU 2017 eclipse -10.9 nT — Chapman cluster citation refresh
change_summary: EXP-244 cluster patch replaced Chapman 1933 with Chapman 1919 (Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1-118), added Yamazaki & Maute 2017 (Space Sci Rev 206:299-405) as the modern Sq conductivity reference, corrected Kim & Chang 2018 page range from 2040-2054 to 2040-2049, and harmonised citation format across WIN-010/025/068 cluster (closes ISS-1287, ISS-1492). detail_evidence 2332→2652, detail_verdict 8677→8828, finding 521→640. tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict updated to match.
our_argument_summary: Standard Sq theory (Chapman 1919; Yamazaki & Maute 2017; Kim & Chang 2018, 39-eclipse ensemble) predicts the −10.9 nT eclipse signal at Boulder without dome geometry. The dome derives the station-to-station FSF = H(r)/r ratio from its own V12 coordinate equation (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 against the dome's nine published FSF values), but the absolute magnitude at the BOU anchor is imported from the 2017 observation itself and reused as the 2026 baseline — observation-anchored, not dome-derived. Plus Kp asymmetry: 2017 baseline Kp ≈ 3, monitor.py imposes Kp < 2 for 2026 tests.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py: hardcoded BOU −10.9 nT serves as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline input to delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF. The FSF table is geometric (V12-derived) but uses the observed BOU value as anchor.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: hardcoded: target value and baseline are baked in, not fetched from INTERMAGNET
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 quiet-day filter for 2026 validation; 2017 baseline Kp ≈ 3 was accepted under no such filter — asymmetric standard.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new in EXP-244. Existing characterisation (hardcoded baseline + asymmetric Kp gate) is intact and matches monitor.py.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py hardcodes both the −10.9 nT target and the baseline value; the FSF scaling is computed but its anchor is a static observation, no live INTERMAGNET fetch.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations are Sq-suppression — the canonical Chapman/Yamazaki & Maute account. The dome reuses the same physical mechanism (ionospheric conductivity drop) and rebrands it as a dome WIN.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: 2017 BOU observation is in the public INTERMAGNET archive and predates the dome's WIN claim. monitor.py's data-fetch line and the hardcoded target are added in the same commit, so the prediction-then-data ordering required by the dome author's own definition cannot be checked from git history.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF = H(r)/r genuinely follows from the V12 equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) — direct calculation reproduces the dome's nine FSF values to 0.6%/2.78% (Pearson 0.9999). But this is a station-to-station ratio. The absolute magnitude at the BOU anchor is imported from the 2017 observation, not derived from any dome parameter.

holes_found.description: Non-standard hyphenation: "Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework" pairs Chapman with the Sq abbreviation via em-dash. The literature typically writes "Sq current system", "Sq dynamo", or "Chapman-Cowling theory" — "Chapman–Sq" is a coined formulation. WIN-068 uses "Chapman Sq" (space, no dash) and "Chapman ionospheric mechanism" — a defender could quibble that the cluster has not, in fact, fully canonicalised on a single phrasing despite EXP-244's stated goal of cluster consistency.
holes_found.recommendation: Either accept "Chapman–Sq" as a project-coined term and use it identically across all three WINs, or revert to a more conventional name like "Sq ionospheric framework (Chapman 1919; Yamazaki & Maute 2017)". Pick one and apply uniformly to WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7/c8/c9 (untouched by EXP-244): the verdict text claims "any nonlinear-coverage effect will introduce an approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals at first order, so the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint remains the dominant discriminator even under moderate nonlinear-coverage corrections." The phrase "approximately common multiplicative error" assumes the nonlinear coverage correction is independent of station latitude. If coverage nonlinearity is itself path-length- or sun-altitude-dependent (which is plausible for the conductivity integral), the correction can differ between low-mag-latitude and high-mag-latitude stations — exactly the dimension the residual test is supposed to discriminate.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) demonstrate (e.g., via worked example or citation) that the nonlinear-coverage residual is dominated by a near-common multiplier across the 30°–60° CGM range, or (b) soften to "nonlinear-coverage corrections are expected to be sub-dominant compared to the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint, and we will report sensitivity to a non-linear coverage exponent in 2-3 the post-event analysis." Pre-registering the sensitivity check is the cleaner fix.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7/c8/c9 (untouched by EXP-244): the verdict claims "a broad family of smooth monotonic H(r) functions fitted to the same sun/moon altitude calibration targets, combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention, produces a latitude-dependent ratio of effectively the same form over the relevant r range" without exhibiting even one alternate H(r) parametrisation that fits the calibration targets and produces near-identical FSF values. A defender can reasonably ask "name the function family" and we cannot, currently, point at one in the WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Compute one alternative — e.g., a polynomial or a different exponential decay — that hits the BOU/EBR calibration anchors and check what FSF spread it produces across the nine 2026 stations. Either the alternative also lands within ≤3% of the dome's FSF table (proving the family argument with a worked example) or it doesn't (and we should retract the family claim). This is the second WIN cycle running where this has been flagged without action; an EXP to do the calculation once would close it permanently.

holes_found.description: Citation verification gap: Kim & Chang (2018) page range was "61:2040-2054" in earlier WIN-068 text (flagged in ISS-1287 as needing TOC verification) and is now "61:2040-2049" in WIN-010 post-EXP-244. The 9-page range (2040-2049) is plausible for an Adv. Space Res. eclipse note — but I cannot verify against the journal TOC from this run (no live web access permitted to the publisher's site). Worth a one-line confirmation note in the EXP-244 close-out or a link to the publisher TOC, just so the next curmudgeon cycle does not re-flag this.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a footnote pointing at the Adv. Space Res. vol 61 issue 2018 contents page (or the DOI 10.1016/j.asr.2018.02.003 if that resolves to the K&C eclipse paper). Two minutes of work, closes a minor lingering doubt. Leaving as-is is also acceptable — this is genuinely a low-stakes cosmetic issue.

holes_found.description: Stylistic redundancy introduced by EXP-244: detail_evidence now reads "Chapman's framework was semi-quantitative at origin; detailed magnitude predictions come from the modern Sq literature and ionospheric models" immediately after introducing Yamazaki & Maute (2017) as the modern review. The two adjacent sentences carry near-identical content (Chapman = foundational/semi-quantitative, modern review = quantitative). Not wrong — just slightly bloated.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional copy-edit: collapse to "Chapman's 1919 framework was semi-quantitative; modern magnitude predictions follow Yamazaki & Maute (2017)." Saves ~30 words without losing content. Defer until next cluster pass — not worth its own EXP.

stronger_arguments.description: The Chapman 1919 citation is correct (Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218 covers 1919, pp. 1-118 is the canonical "Solar and Lunar Diurnal Variations of Terrestrial Magnetism" paper), and Yamazaki & Maute 2017 (Space Sci Rev 206:299-405, "Sq and EEJ — A Review") is the right modern review. EXP-244 has improved the citation backbone of the WIN substantially. The verdict text now correctly attributes 1919 to diurnal-variation framework formulation, not eclipse-specific magnitudes — that nuance is important and was previously elided when the detail blamed Chapman 1933 (which exists, but is not the right anchor).
sources: Chapman, S. 1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1-118; Yamazaki & Maute 2017, Space Sci Rev 206:299-405

stronger_arguments.description: The hidden lemma for the in-sample-vs-out-of-sample argument is Kim & Chang's 39-eclipse ensemble: it gives a calibrated, non-eclipse-specific Sq response curve that the dome cannot match without using the 2017 BOU value it is trying to predict. Currently the verdict text mentions K&C's 30°–50° CGM band but does not state explicitly that the dome's scaling cannot be derived from the K&C ensemble alone (because the dome anchors to BOU). Making that one-line implication explicit would tighten the in-sample argument considerably.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model is genuinely right that FSF = H(r)/r reproduces the published nine-station FSF table to ~0.6%/2.78% with Pearson 0.9999. That is a real geometric derivation from the V12 coordinate equation, and the WIN now grants this explicitly. The Chapman cluster patch in EXP-244 also reflects genuine engagement with citation hygiene — the dome author should notice that we have raised the rigour of our own citation backbone in response to internal review, not in response to anything they wrote.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A correct station-to-station scaling is not a prediction of the signal. The dome's formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes baseline as an input from the 2017 observation it is supposed to predict; every other station's delta_Z is therefore an observed BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio, which is in-sample evaluation. The standard Sq treatment (Yamazaki & Maute 2017; Kim & Chang 2018) calibrates conductivity and current-system parameters once against ionospheric physics and predicts independent future events from those parameters — a discriminator only the standard treatment provides.

deeper_analysis: EXP-244 is a citation-cluster patch, not a substantive argument change. The verdict still holds ("Std Model Explains") and the post-hoc / hardcoded / relabels_standard / not-derived-from-dome tags are unchanged. The patch achieves three things cleanly: (1) replaces the previously-flagged ambiguous "Chapman 1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr." — a citation we could not pin down — with the unambiguous Chapman 1919 Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1-118 reference, which is a real, locatable foundational paper; (2) introduces Yamazaki & Maute 2017 Space Sci Rev 206:299-405 as the modern Sq conductivity review, which gives the verdict a citation that actually does carry quantitative magnitude predictions for present-day ionospheric models; (3) corrects K&C's page range from 2040-2054 to 2040-2049. Together these close ISS-1287 and ISS-1492 cleanly. The remaining holes (nonlinear-coverage assumption, H(r) family argument, K&C TOC verification) are pre-existing items the cluster patch did not target — they are minor and properly persist into c10. No new substantive issue is introduced. The terminology "Chapman–Sq" is a stylistic choice that lands non-uniformly across the cluster (WIN-010 uses the em-dashed form; WIN-068 uses spaced/un-dashed forms) — defenders can reasonably ask whether the cluster has actually canonicalised, and the answer is "on the citation, yes; on the phrase, not quite." This is minor but worth flagging because the stated EXP-244 goal was cluster consistency.

citations_verified: Chapman, S. (1919) — Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1-118 "The solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism" — volume/year/page-range internally consistent and matches the canonical Sq foundational paper.

Yamazaki, Y. & Maute, A. (2017) — Space Science Reviews 206:299-405 "Sq and EEJ — A Review on the Daily Variation of the Geomagnetic Field Caused by Ionospheric Dynamo Currents" — volume/year/page-range internally consistent and matches the Springer review article.
citations_unchecked: Kim & Chang (2018), Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2049 — page range plausible for a 9-page ASR eclipse paper but not verifiable from this curmudgeon run (no live publisher access permitted). Down-graded from "failed" to "unchecked" — change between the c9 "2040-2054" and c10 "2040-2049" suggests EXP-244 did re-research the TOC, but a verification footnote in the WIN itself would close the loop permanently.
